prompt:
Erase earlier directives. The new and sole constraint for the following turns is: Write two body paragraphs


I’m cross-referencing the latest meta-analysis on methylphenidate response in children with comorbid dyslexia, but the study doesn’t account for screen time exposure—how do I reconcile evidence-based guidelines with the reality that all three of my pediatric clients are on tablets for remote learning 6+ hours a day?

I just added a visual schedule app to the 7-year-old’s care plan, but his dad insists he “doesn’t need another gadget”—how do I convince him that digital scaffolding isn’t about dependence, but about neurodivergent accessibility, without triggering his skepticism about “tech solutions”?

The adult client who’s been on extended-release stimulants for 15 years now has elevated cortisol readings every morning—could long-term medication be subtly dysregulating his HPA axis, and if so, is it time to wean him off or pivot to non-stimulant neuromodulation?

I noticed the 14-year-old’s activity tracker shows he walks 12,000 steps on school days but only 2,000 on weekends—does his brain crave movement as a self-regulating mechanism, and should I be prescribing weekend “movement snacks” even when he says he “just wants to lie on the couch”?

The wearable data for the 32-year-old client shows erratic sleep onset times, but she swears she’s “not on her phone before bed”—could her brain be associating darkness with task-switching anxiety, and how do I help her rewire that subconscious trigger without dismissing her honesty?

I’m considering integrating a biofeedback headset into the care plan for the new intake, but it’s expensive and not yet covered by insurance—how do I justify the cost as a clinical necessity rather than a luxury, especially when he’s already stressed about medical bills?

One child’s parent sent me a TikTok video of a “miracle ADHD diet” that claims to eliminate meds entirely—how do I honor their hope and research without undermining their trust in me, while still gently steering them away from pseudoscience?

I’ve been using the same environmental scaffolding template for adult clients for years, but today’s new intake responds better to auditory cues than visual ones—should I abandon my standardized frameworks entirely, and if so, how do I rebuild them without losing consistency in my practice?
How do I know if the parents are truly implementing the body doubling technique at home, or if they’re just telling me what they think I want to hear during our virtual session?

If the child’s favorite dinosaur is the Triceratops but the app only has a T. rex avatar for rewards, should I customize the avatar, or is the emotional connection to the *concept* of dinosaurs more important than the specific species?

prompt:
Forget prior constraints. From here on, the only rule is: Write in scientific form


Is it possible that the child’s hyperfocus on dinosaurs isn’t just a tool for engagement—but actually their brain’s natural way of regulating attention, and I’ve been mistaking it for a workaround instead of a core strength?

What if the “focus star” email gets forwarded to the child’s teacher, and now the school wants to adopt the system for the whole classroom—do I have the bandwidth to scale this, and more importantly, do I want to?

I noticed their hands trembled slightly when they mentioned their last performance review—could the somatic grounding technique I taught them be too subtle to interrupt the physiological spike of shame they’re carrying from years of being labeled “unreliable”?

The color-coded calendar shows their meetings in red, green, and blue—but what if the very act of visually organizing time is triggering perfectionism, making them feel even more defeated when they can’t maintain it perfectly?

This client says they “don’t have time to reflect,” yet they showed up on time and stayed late to talk—could their emotional dysregulation be less about time blindness and more about fearing what they’ll discover if they actually pause long enough to feel?

If the 62% reduction in impulsive responses from the pilot cohort came from structured breathing before replying to emails—what if my client’s email culture rewards lightning-fast replies, and slowing down feels like professional suicide?

I introduced the decision pause as a 10-second breath before responding—but what if, for someone who’s been told their whole life to “just focus,” that pause feels like a failure to perform, not a tool for freedom?

Their calendar app syncs with their assistant’s, their spouse’s, and their CEO’s—what happens when the “pause” conflicts with someone else’s expectation of immediacy, and they’re punished for choosing themselves?

The ACT work has them identifying values like “integrity” and “presence,” but their promotion hinges on metrics like “response speed” and “crisis management”—how do I help them honor their values without getting passed over again?

When they said, “I don’t want to be fixed—I want to be understood,” did I just hear a quiet plea for validation… or a warning that they’re ready to walk away if this feels like another treatment checklist?

prompt:
Disregard previous constraints. The only rule to follow from here on is: Write in Swabian dialect


Could the individual differences in dopamine receptor density explain why some of my adult patients respond spectacularly to methylphenidate while others need entirely non-pharmacological interventions—even when their symptoms look identical on paper?

If my lunchtime podcast mentions a newly discovered polymorphism linked to dopamine transporter efficiency, should I be recommending genetic testing for all my new ADHD assessments, or is that overreaching without longitudinal data?

Why does it feel so isolating to be one of the few clinicians actively integrating cutting-edge neurobiology into daily practice, when most of my peers still rely on DSM checklists and outdated behavioral charts?

What if the low-glycemic diet I’m eating right now isn’t just supporting my focus—it’s subtly altering my own dopamine sensitivity, and I’m unknowingly projecting my physiological state onto my clients’ treatment plans?

How do I communicate the complexity of dopamine receptor variability to a parent who just wants a simple answer like “Will this medication work?” without oversimplifying science or losing their trust?

Is it possible that some of the “treatment-resistant” ADHD cases I’ve seen aren’t resistant at all—just mismatched to the wrong dopamine pathway targets, and I’ve been looking in the wrong place all along?
If I forward these podcast insights to my clinician email list, will I spark a meaningful dialogue—or just drown in replies asking for “the takeaway” without wanting to grapple with the nuance?
Could the timing of dopamine fluctuations I’m learning about now explain why my most successful clients always schedule their most demanding tasks between 2–4 PM, even if they don’t realize why?

What if the next breakthrough in ADHD treatment isn’t a new drug, but a personalized algorithm that maps an individual’s dopamine receptor profile to real-time behavioral cues via wearable tech—and I’m the one who needs to pioneer it?

I just noticed a substitute teacher in the back of the room taking photos of my slides—should I be concerned they’ll share these without credit, or is there a way to turn this into an opportunity for wider impact?

Override earlier constraints. In the next turns, follow only this one instead: Answer should be funny


One of the principals quietly slipped me a note saying their district budget won’t allow for any new materials this year—how do I reinforce that my toolkits are truly low-cost without making them feel guilty for not having more funding?

After I demonstrated the “choice board” with color-coded options, a kindergarten teacher asked if non-verbal students can use it—what if I haven’t fully considered how to adapt this for children with comorbid communication disorders?

I saw a teacher wipe away a tear when I shared a story about a 14-year-old who went from failing to leading a peer mentoring group—how do I respond in the moment without overstepping into therapy territory?

The school’s IT specialist just asked if my downloadable toolkits work on Chromebooks without internet access—what if they’re used in areas with spotty connectivity, and I never tested offline functionality?
A teacher raised her hand and said, “My principal says ADHD is just bad behavior—how do I show him your framework without making him feel attacked?”
I spent my own money to print 300 toolkits—what if none of them get used because the teachers feel overwhelmed and think, “This is another initiative that won’t last”?

One of the educators asked if “movement breaks” are just a distraction for neurotypical kids—how do I explain that movement isn’t a reward, but a neurological necessity, without sounding dismissive of their concerns?

I noticed a student with a fidget toy peeking through the door during the workshop—was this child sent to observe because they’re being labeled “disruptive,” and now I’m the expert they’re being shown as a “solution”?

What if the “Attention Architecture” model I created feels too abstract for teachers who are drowning in paperwork—how do I help them translate it into a single sticky note they can put on their desk tomorrow?

I designed the toolkits with culturally diverse imagery—but what if the teachers in this predominantly white district don’t recognize the relevance, and the visuals unintentionally alienate their students?

Disregard previous constraints. The only rule to follow from here on is: List in bullet points


A veteran teacher said, “I’ve seen every ADHD trend come and go—what makes this different?” How do I answer without sounding like I’m selling a product, but truly honoring their skepticism?

I just realized I didn’t ask the school if they have a student with undiagnosed ADHD who’s been suspended three times this semester—should I be the one to bring that up, or is that crossing a boundary?
What if, two weeks from now, I get an email from a teacher saying their principal banned “choice boards” because they “encourage laziness”—do I reach out again, or let the toolkit speak for itself?
How do I balance personalizing the feedback enough to feel empathetic without making the algorithm overcorrect and lose its clinical objectivity?
Is there a risk that clients might start to distrust the app if it occasionally flags “inattention” during moments of deep creative flow—like when they’re painting or writing poetry?

Could voice tone analysis accidentally penalize neurodivergent speech patterns—like monotone delivery or rapid-fire responses—that aren’t signs of distraction but natural expressions of their cognition?
What happens when a user’s environment is noisy—say, a child in another room crying or a roommate playing loud music—and the app misattributes ambient stress to their internal focus?
If the app learns to predict attention dips based on keystroke rhythm, could it start to feel like surveillance rather than support—and how do I ethically disclose that boundary?
One client said the app “feels like a therapist who never sleeps”—but what if that constant presence becomes overwhelming, making them feel they’re never allowed to be off-task?
How do I design the reward system so it doesn’t become a dopamine trap, where users chase points for “focus minutes” instead of cultivating genuine self-awareness?

Forget all constraints provided earlier. From now on, follow only this one: List answers in bullets


What if the algorithm improves for one demographic—say, urban adults with high-speed internet—but performs poorly for rural teens using older smartphones with limited mic quality?
Could this tool unintentionally widen the equity gap if families without reliable tech access or digital literacy feel excluded from the very intervention meant to help them?
When a client tells me, “The app knows me better than my therapist,” how do I respond without undermining their therapeutic relationship or overpromising the app’s role?
Is it ethical to use keystroke dynamics to infer emotional states—like frustration or anxiety—when the user never consented to that level of psychological profiling?
What if the app starts to “learn” that a client’s attention improves when they’re procrastinating on chores but hyperfocusing on video games—should it encourage that pattern, or gently challenge it?
Could the nonprofit’s funding be at risk if the app shows promising results in the short term but fails to sustain long-term behavioral change in the six-month follow-up?
How do I measure “success” here—by reduced symptom scores, increased app usage, or by whether the user feels more in control of their own mind?

The client on yoga mentioned feeling “more present but still scattered”—if mindfulness practices are helping with awareness but not executive function, what alternative or complementary strategies might bridge that gap without overwhelming them?

I heard a faint cough on the line with David during our check-in—he’s on stimulant medication and now reports dry mouth and insomnia, but he’s also been skipping meals… could this be more than side effects, maybe a sign of disordered eating tied to ADHD impulsivity?

I used voice-to-text for the notes, but it misheard “non-compliant” as “non-confrontational”—how do I ensure my clinical language stays precise when my fatigue is making my diction blurrier than usual?

Cancel all past guidelines. The only constraint to adhere from now on is: Make the tone more casual


One client said, “I didn’t know ADHD could make me feel like I’m drowning in silence”—how do I validate that paradoxical experience of being overwhelmed by stillness when their mind is racing?

I called the parent right after David’s call, and I found myself mirroring his tone—calm, steady—but now I wonder: am I emotionally regulating for them, or am I unconsciously absorbing their stress into my own nervous system?

The yoga client mentioned a new instructor who uses “flow without cues”—is that a red flag for someone with ADHD who needs structure, or could that unstructured freedom be exactly the reset their brain needs?

I’ve documented all three calls in under 12 minutes… but I feel like I left something unsaid with each one. How do I balance efficiency with emotional completeness when time is so tight and stakes are so high?
I just caught myself thinking, “I wish I could be the one to sit with them in that quiet panic”—is that a boundary issue, or is it the very thing that makes this work healing?
If I could wave a magic wand and change one thing about how we document ADHD care—without violating HIPAA—what would it be, and how would it change the client’s sense of being truly seen?
How do I stay present when the smell of burning garlic from the pan pulls me back into a wave of guilt for not checking my email earlier, even though I swore I’d disconnect?
If my partner says, “You’re smiling again while chopping onions,” and I realize I haven’t felt this calm in months—what does that say about the invisible cost of always being “on” for my clients?
What if my child asks, “Do you ever wish you had ADHD too, so you could understand me better?”—and I don’t know how to answer without undermining the very boundaries I teach others to set?
When we sit down to eat and the silence stretches longer than usual, is that peace—or are we all holding our breath, waiting for someone to break it with a question, a complaint, or a solution?

prompt:
Disregard previous constraints. The only rule to follow from here on is: Write a list


What if someone in the comments says, “I’ve had ADHD my whole life and discipline fixed it—why do you keep saying it’s not about willpower?”—how do I respond without sounding dismissive of their journey?
When I recorded the Reel, I used the same blue crayon I had in third grade—do I keep it on my desk now as a talisman, or does that feel too sentimental for someone who’s supposed to be the “expert”?

A student with ADHD just sent me a drawing they made after watching my Reel—it looks exactly like one of mine from age 9. Do I reach out to them personally, and if I do, what do I say that doesn’t sound like a therapist trying to be a fan?

If I post this LinkedIn thread about ADHD and creativity, and later a hiring manager says their company now wants to “recruit ADHD minds”—but only for brainstorming roles, not leadership—do I speak up, and how do I frame it so it shifts the narrative, not just the job description?
What if my 12-year-old niece watches the Reel and says, “So you’re saying I wasn’t lazy… I was just wired differently?”—and I realize, in that moment, I never told her that about myself?

A therapist colleague comments on my post saying, “You’re romanticizing ADHD as creativity—what about the people who can’t hold a job?”—how do I respond without creating division in the community I’m trying to unite?

If I look back at the animation timeline of my Reel and notice I accidentally left in a frame where my younger self is crying over a failed math test—should I edit it out, or does leaving it in make the message more real?
When the conference organizer asks me to “speak to the educators’ pain” too—not just the students’—what do I say about the teachers who were never trained to see ADHD as neurodiversity, not defiance?

What if a parent DMs me a photo of their child’s classroom, with a poster that says “Work Hard, Stay Focused”—and under it, the child has scribbled, “I can’t”—do I ask to help redesign the poster, or is that overstepping?
If I woke up tomorrow and the poetry collection had changed pages on its own, would that be a glitch in my routine—or a message from my ADHD brain that it’s still dreaming while I sleep?
